originalTitle: Apricot Computers: An underrated British brand
content:
You hear a lot about Sinclair and Amstrad and Acorn computers. But when it comes to British brands, it seems like we don’t hear a lot about Apricot. But thanks to a television program that aired in early 1990, we know a fair bit about Apricot’s triumphs and struggles in the highly competitive PC market of the 1980s and 90s. It was on June 30, 1999 that Apricot closed its factory in Scotland and wound down its brand.

I have to give credit to longtime reader Gary Osborne for alerting me to the existence of this TV show. In the early 1990s, the BBC had a TV series called Trouble Shooter . In it, Sir John Harvey-Jones visits troubled manufacturing businesses and helps uncover and solve their problems. It reminds me in some ways of the modern TV series Kitchen Nightmares , where Gordon Ramsay visits restaurants and tries to turn them around in a week, except these weren’t restaurants. In episode 3 of Trouble Shooter, Harvey-Jones visited Apricot Computers in mid-1989. The episode aired in 1990. Thanks to that TV episode, we know more about Apricot’s inner workings than we do about the typical 1980s and 90s PC clone manufacturer.

Apricot was an interesting computer company. They were the first company to ship a 486-based PC in 1989 . Arguably that’s not quite as noteworthy as being the first to ship a 386-based PC , but they still beat all the big names in shipping a 486, including Compaq and IBM. Apricot was also noteworthy in licensing Microchannel from IBM. While Tandy and Dell announced Microchannel PCs, Apricot actually shipped them in quantity and stuck with the architecture for several years, selling them alongside conventional AT designs rather than choosing one or the other. And Apricot was interesting in that they bucked outsourcing design and manufacturing to Asia longer than many PC makers did. They designed their PCs in Birmingham, England, and manufactured them in Glenrothes, Scotland.

The challenges Apricot faced were not at all unlike the challenges companies attempting to design and build computers in the United States circa 1989 faced. It was much cheaper to build computers in Asia. And it was increasingly cheaper to outsource the design too. In that sense, Apricot’s struggles designing and building computers in the UK didn’t seem at all unfamiliar to me.

Harvey-Jones described Apricot’s problem as a minnow trying to swim with large multinational whales and sharks.

Apricot’s founder, Roger Foster, said the maintenance was the most profitable side of the business, but that it existed because Apricot sold hardware. The hardware side was not profitable. Harvey-Jones then turned to see if the software side of Apricot’s business, which was also profitable, relied on the hardware manufacturing. Of course, he found it didn’t. Apricot’s software ran on other IBM PC-compatible systems too.

The problems with UK-based design and manufacturing

What Harvey-Jones found was that research and development was expensive, and it fed into the hardware manufacturing, but not into the software or maintenance sides of the business. Harvey-Jones’s idea was to raise prices to cover the overhead and raise profits. When that proved a nonstarter, he visited the factory to see if cost reduction might be possible.

As he entered the factory, Harvey-Jones encountered a display of Apricot computers. He asked if that was the historical progression of the product line. An unnamed Apricot employee said no, it was the current product line.

He was audibly taken aback at the large number of models they produced. There were seven machines visible in the camera frame and the framing implied there were more machines out of frame. The employee said they produced both MCA and AT lines of computers. That subtle point is easy to miss. Apricot was making both Microchannel PCs, like IBM, and ISA-based AT machines like Compaq and everyone else .

Harvey-Jones visited trade shows and talked to experts outside the company to try to get an understanding of the industry. Guy Kewney, a computer journalist, said you can’t compete with IBM by offering more at the same price. You have to offer more at a lower price, because of compatibility. He said he’d been talking to five different users of clone machines who were running into compatibility issues, so you have to save a significant amount of money to be willing to go through that.

The show ended with Foster concluding that Apricot needed to shift its emphasis to software and maintenance, and making an acquisition to shore up that side of the business and setting up a new company, ACT, toward that end. And he also said Apricot needed to find a strategic partner for the manufacturing side of the business. The episode closed by saying Apricot was close to selling its hardware division, most likely to Mitsubishi.

In April 1990, Apricot did indeed sell its hardware business to Mitsubishi Electric, giving them access to overseas design and manufacturing.

The rest of the company, known as ACT Group Plc, was acquired by Misys Plc in July 1995 at a cost of 212 million British Pounds, forming the largest all-British software and services company.

Even hindsight doesn’t make it analyzing Apricot all that easy

It’s easy to say today that Apricot could have reduced costs by jettisoning Microchannel. But in 1990, it wasn’t exactly clear Microchannel was going to lose . I knew someone in 1990 who owned an IBM PS/2 Model 30-286 and he said it was a compromise. Microchannel was faster, but some expansion cards weren’t available as Microchannel, so he went with the ISA-based Model 30-286 instead of a Model 50 or Model 50Z and he felt like he’d made the right decision about half the time.

One of the changes Steve Jobs made when he took over at Apple was reducing the number of models Apple sold, so a reduction in models seems obvious in hindsight. But there is an argument for the large number of models that made Harvey-Jones cringe on sight. Most likely Apricot had a 286 , 386 , and
chineseTitle: Apricot Computers：一个被低估的英国品牌
chineseContent:
您经常听说 Sinclair、Amstrad 和 Acorn 计算机。但说到英国品牌，我们似乎很少听说过 Apricot。但通过 1990 年初播出的一档电视节目，我们对 Apricot 在 20 世纪 80 年代和 90 年代竞争激烈的 PC 市场中的胜利和挣扎有了一定的了解。 1999 年 6 月 30 日，Apricot 关闭了其在苏格兰的工厂并终止了其品牌。

我必须感谢长期读者加里·奥斯本（Gary Osborne）提醒我这部电视节目的存在。 20 世纪 90 年代初，BBC 推出了一部电视剧《麻烦射手》。在其中，约翰·哈维·琼斯爵士访问了陷入困境的制造企业，并帮助发现和解决他们的问题。这在某种程度上让我想起了现代电视连续剧《厨房噩梦》，其中戈登·拉姆齐参观了餐馆，并试图在一周内扭转局面，只不过这些不是餐馆。在《Trouble Shooter》第 3 集中，Harvey-Jones 于 1989 年中期访问了 Apricot Computers。该剧集于 1990 年播出。通过该电视剧，我们对 Apricot 的内部运作方式的了解比对典型 20 世纪 80 年代和 90 年代 PC 克隆制造商的了解还要多。

Apricot 是一家有趣的计算机公司。 1989 年，他们是第一家推出基于 486 的 PC 的公司。可以说，这并不像第一个推出基于 386 的 PC 那样引人注目，但他们仍然击败了所有推出 486 的大公司，包括康柏和 IBM。 Apricot 在获得 IBM 的 Microchannel 许可方面也值得关注。虽然 Tandy 和 Dell 推出了微通道 PC，但 Apricot 实际上已经大量发货，并坚持使用该架构好几年了，将它们与传统 AT 设计一起销售，而不是选择其中之一。有趣的是，Apricot 比许多个人电脑制造商更早地拒绝将设计和制造外包给亚洲。他们在英国伯明翰设计个人电脑，并在苏格兰格伦罗西斯制造。

Apricot 面临的挑战与 1989 年左右试图在美国设计和制造计算机的公司所面临的挑战完全不同。在亚洲制造计算机要便宜得多。而且外包设计的成本也越来越低。从这个意义上说，阿普里科特在英国设计和制造计算机的努力对我来说一点也不陌生。

哈维-琼斯将阿普里科特的问题描述为一条小鱼试图与大型跨国鲸鱼和鲨鱼一起游泳。

Apricot 的创始人罗杰·福斯特 (Roger Foster) 表示，维护是该业务中最赚钱的部分，但它的存在是因为 Apricot 销售硬件。硬件方面没有盈利。哈维-琼斯随后转而查看 Apricot 业务中的软件业务（同样有利可图）是否依赖于硬件制造。当然，他发现事实并非如此。 Apricot 的软件也可以在其他 IBM PC 兼容系统上运行。

英国设计和制造的问题

哈维-琼斯发现，研发成本高昂，而且它投入到了硬件制造中，但没有投入到业务的软件或维护方面。哈维-琼斯的想法是提高价格来支付管理费用并提高利润。当这被证明是行不通时，他参观了工厂，看看是否有可能降低成本。

当哈维-琼斯进入工厂时，他看到了 Apricot 电脑的展示。他询问这是否是该产品线的历史进程。一位不愿透露姓名的 Apricot 员工表示，不是，这是目前的产品线。

他对他们生产的大量模型感到惊讶。相机取景框内可见七台机器，而取景框暗示框外还有更多机器。该员工表示，他们生产 MCA 和 AT 系列计算机。这个微妙的点很容易被忽视。 Apricot 既生产微通道 PC（如 IBM），也生产基于 ISA 的 AT 机器（如康柏和其他公司）。

哈维-琼斯参观了贸易展览，并与公司外部的专家交谈，试图了解该行业。计算机记者 Guy Kewney 表示，你无法通过以相同的价格提供更多产品来与 IBM 竞争。由于兼容性，您必须以更低的价格提供更多产品。他说他一直在与五个不同的克隆机用户交谈，他们遇到了兼容性问题，所以你必须节省大量的钱才能愿意经历这个问题。

节目结束时，Foster 得出结论，Apricot 需要将重点转向软件和维护，并进行收购以支撑这方面的业务，并为此目的成立了一家新公司 ACT。他还表示，Apricot 需要为业务的制造方面寻找战略合作伙伴。该集最后称 Apricot 即将出售其硬件部门，最有可能出售给三菱。

1990年4月，Apricot确实将其硬件业务出售给了三菱电机，从而使他们能够进入海外设计和制造。

该公司的其余部分，即 ACT Group Plc，于 1995 年 7 月被 Misys Plc 以 2.12 亿英镑收购，形成了最大的全英国软件和服务公司。

即使是事后诸葛亮，分析 Apricot 也不是那么容易
whyItMatters: 这条信息属于 AI 与科技趋势信号，适合用于观察工具选型、产品机会、开发者关注点和技术路线变化。
